serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of French Bean with Dried Shrimp as a side dish in a Singaporean Chinese meal is approximately 150 g, representing a shared plate portion.

nutrient_source.title: French Beans (Snap Beans), cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (USDA SR Legacy 11053) and Shrimp, dried (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175180), combined with HPB Singapore nutrient reference for stir-fried vegetable dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for French Bean with Dried Shrimp as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked French beans (FDC 11053) and dried shrimp (FDC 175180) in approximate proportions (85% French beans, 10% dried shrimp, 5% oil/seasoning), cross-referenced with HPB Singapore stir-fried vegetable dish profiles. Energy and macros are calculated from component contributions. Sodium is elevated due to dried shrimp and soy sauce seasoning.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — green beans / French beans entry; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 32 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for green beans / French beans (GI ~32, low category). The dried shrimp component contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI of the dish. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 9.6) / 100 ≈ 3.1, where carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 6.4 × 1.5 = 9.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from dried shrimp and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with portion size as the sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) from dried shrimp and soy sauce can contribute significantly to daily sodium limits; requesting reduced soy sauce or rinsing dried shrimp before cooking can lower sodium by 20–30%.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from French beans
type2_diabetes.guidance: French Bean with Dried Shrimp has a low glycemic index (~32) and provides approximately 2.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g, making it a suitable vegetable side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it supports glycaemic control and satiety without causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from dried shrimp
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While French beans provide beneficial dietary fibre and low saturated fat, the dried shrimp component contributes cholesterol (~28 mg per 100 g) and high sodium (~420 mg per 100 g), which warrants moderation for individuals managing cardiovascular disease; choosing a lower-sodium preparation with reduced dried shrimp quantity is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: French Bean with Dried Shrimp (虾米四季豆, Xia Mi Si Ji Dou) is a classic Chinese stir-fried vegetable dish popular in Singapore and Southern Chinese cuisine. The dish consists of fresh French beans (also known as green beans or snap beans) stir-fried at high heat with dried shrimp (hae bee / 虾米), garlic, and light seasoning such as soy sauce and salt. The cooking method is rapid stir-frying in a wok, which preserves the crisp texture and bright green colour of the beans while infusing them with the umami-rich, slightly briny flavour of the dried shrimp. The result is a savoury, crunchy side dish with a pronounced seafood aroma. Nutritionally, this dish is relatively low in calories (~72 kcal per 100 g) and carbohydrates (~6.4 g per 100 g), with a meaningful protein contribution (~5.2 g per 100 g) from the dried shrimp. It provides dietary fibre (~2.8 g per 100 g) from the French beans, supporting digestive health and glycaemic control. The primary nutritional concern is sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) from the dried shrimp and soy sauce, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is low (~32), making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as a shared side dish in Chinese family-style meals, zi char restaurants, and seafood establishments. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or individuals with shellfish or crustacean allergies.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for French Bean with Dried Shrimp as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked French beans and dried shrimp in approximate proportions, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore stir-fried vegetable dish profiles. GI is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for French beans/green beans as the dominant carbohydrate-contributing ingredient. Confidence is medium due to the estimated composite nature of the nutrient profile.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data (USDA SR Legacy: cooked French beans FDC 11053, dried shrimp FDC 175180) in approximate proportions of 85% French beans, 10% dried shrimp, 5% oil and seasoning, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore stir-fried vegetable dish profiles. Sodium is elevated due to dried shrimp and soy sauce; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries for these ingredients. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as confirmed negligible in plant-based stir-fried dishes per USDA data. GI of 32 is from the University of Sydney GI Database for French beans/green beans as the primary carbohydrate source; the dried shrimp component contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the dish GI. Halal status is marked false as dried shrimp sourcing and preparation cannot be confirmed as halal-certified without restaurant-specific verification. Food group reclassified from input 'Fish and seafood' to 'Vegetables' as French beans are the dominant ingredient by weight and the dish is primarily a vegetable side dish.
